author: @Chimel31
text: You didn't measure the exhaust temp but it seems to be around 100°C from other videos, quite a lot of wasted heat for a heater. Not sure how best to handle this issue. Redesigning and 3D-printing the radiator so it includes a few loops inside for the exhaust would probably be best, but on the other hand, a hot exhaust adds a secondary heating source that can be used for another room or the greenhouse or for a warm bread dough proofing or yogurt making cabinet, if you insulate the exhaust pipe well enough until that secondary heated location, then adds a few coils of copper there before releasing the now cold exhaust air outdoors.

Hi Rob, Nice Video, Got one of these in my garage, I find it works well on diesel, kerosene, Old engine oil and kerosene 50/50, Old or new veg oil and kerosene, just filter the old stuff.
To be honest i expect it would work on petrol, methional and ethanol but haven't had the balls to try it.
If you use old engine or veg oil you need to run it flat out on kerosene every now and then to burn off soot build up in the combustion chamber.

author: @keithbill310
text: So  how long does a 12 volt 100Ah  battery last before it needs charging when its  connected to one of these heaters ?   and how long does the fuel last between fill ups ?

replies.author: @juliogonzo2718
replies.text: I think on low they draw approximately 500ma and high approx 3a. Glow plug on they draw 10a for a minute or so on startup and shutdown. If your battery is lead acid the ah rating is only usable by half so 50ah. Still would go for a long time. The one in my truck i run off a 20ah lifepo4 at 1.8 Hz for about 6 hours at a time and barely makes a dent in the battery. They use .022ml per pump pulse. So when I set mine at 1.8hz it burns 143ml/hour

author: @oliver90owner
text: I’m looking forward to your tests.  Like how much fuel is supplied/hour will provide a measure of its output (after allowing 15-20% for heat lost via the exhaust?).  Diesel fuel contains about 10 1/2kWh of energy…. 8kW will need almost a litre per hour after inefficiencies, I think….

replies.author: @oliver90owner
replies.text: @gotelemwillyboyshere Quite right.  The discrepancies also extend to the 3 and 2kW varieties.  I don’t expect many, at all, comply with the ‘given’ specification - at least when delivered.  Even if fitted with a 28ml/1000 stroke pump an 8kW would need to be pumping at close to 10Hz.  Furthermore the fan for the combustion air would either need to be much larger or run much faster.

author: @TheKlink
text: one of the things i like about rocket mass heater installations is that they draw air from the room and then exhaust it out, that way you constantly have your air refreshed. This is largely without the traditional inefficiencies of a fireplace, because of all the radiant surfaces and heat batteries built into the RMHs.  This heater suffers from the same drawbacks as a minisplit; you're re-cycling the same air, that's how people get sick, whether you clean the filters or not.

replies.author: @johngarbutt
replies.text: Yeah but you don't have to recycle the same air. Just take the air from outside via a duct into wherever the heater is.

author: @ktm42080
text: Excellent! I used to build utility trucks (manlifts for working on poles and the like) and we would install Espar heaters which are a similar idea, just shaped a little different, in the cab of the truck. Fuel line went directly into the diesel tank, intake and exhaust were directed out the cab by flexible metal tubes and the heater itself went under the seat. Really hot and efficient without running the engine. Thanks for another great video!

author: @Route72Trucks
text:
Great video!  I’ll be buying one for sure.

I’ve been wanting to make a oil heater to supplement.  This looks perfect!

replies.author: @PsiQ
replies.text:
Hmm. even as a supplement heater and when used in a leaky farmhouse  .. you will still loose a lot of additional heat energy out the exhaust.
i would suggest adding another (as large as possible) heat exchanger to the exhaust pipe and pump maybe water through that. Simply soldering a few long pipes together should work if long enough. When the exhaust temp at the end of pipes gets down to 65°C or below (dont know that in freedom units)
you extracted most of the energy and even start getting into condensation energy usage. just keep mind of the nasty corrosive water
so the final pipe should go straight up/down so the condensation water can get out through a small hole in the bottom into a container to be neutralized.

author: @brianmurphy8790
text:
When I was a kid my Christmas presents were usually in bits by boxing day.
They often never went back together - never worked again - but at least I knew what was inside. 
I did learn a lot and family learned to by me different presents - screwdrivers, spanners, Allen keys and motor bikes....
I loved taking old TVs to bits. I used to collect the valves, because they looked cool.
When I was 13, I got a Hillman Imp and a big selection of tools.
Yeah, I took the Imp to bits. Wasn't long before I got bored of it and bought a Vauxhall Chevette. I took that to bits too. By that time though, things worked when I put them back together..

I'm currently acquiring parts for my next drip-fed oil burner project - kinda small - the intention is for it to be 'functional art'. 
The plan is to use the cylinder barrel/head from my old Lister LD1 as the secondary burn chamber. There will be at least one stove fan on the the primary chamber pushing air over the fins of the barrel.
Once the burner is made, it's going inside the empty iron case of a Jotul F3 log burner - bolted to my living room wall.

author: @harry130747
text: A dangerous contraption.   What happens to the poisonous combustion gases?

author: @Enjoymentboy
text: These things are rather interesting and coincidentally when I was watching your previous video on the biodeisel powered rocket stove I thought of one of these units.  I was thinking that using one of the cyclic/momentary fuel pumps as these use you could pressurise the pre-heat coil better and might even be able to bet away with a smaller ID tube with fewer turns int he coil.  Possibly even a straight run like with old coleman kerosene stoves.  Just enough to vaporise the fuel.  Since these pumps run on 12V you could, in theory, run it off a car battery connected to a solar charger and have no input energy expense.

replies.author: @robindumpleton3742
replies.text: Don't go any smaller tube, it makes the pump work harder and it cavitates. On RV I've piped with brake pipe

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
My favourite additive to any first aid kit is pottassiam permanganate. 1. It makes water safe to drink,   2. It acts as a disinfectant and 3. When added to an organic liquid like vegetable glycerine it combusts to create a very hot flame to start a good fire. If you vape you can add a few drops of your juice to create the fire 🔥.
Great video Rob, very helpful.

author: @yougeo
text:
I have a similar one. They put out a lot of heat but use a lot of electricity if you use the thermostat which turns the glow plug on and 9ff. If you instead use the constant fuel rate the glow plug stays hot from burning and doesn't use electricity to reheat it. So the only electricity used is the fan.

Makes a big difference.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice tip mate - thanks for sharing

replies.author: @PeterJavea
replies.text:
Mine is a 5kw "all in one" red one.
I am monitoring the current draw.
For the first start up period it draws current.  Then the flame inside starts and this initial current drops instantly to the fan only running. 
I use a deep cycle 12v 22ah capacity battery together with a small trickle charger.  A bit like the car battery starting the car engine and the alternator running things plus recharging the battery again. 
I do this so that if, in my cabin, there's a power outage, the battery will keep it running for hours.
If I had no battery and there were a power outage,  the unit would stop instantly and the cooling fan also.  So the 150-220°C of the burner would directly damage the ICU, etc.  (Worth avoiding)

To microwave, for only a few minutes, then switch back automatically,  maybe a capacitor and relay, simply let the cap discharge into the relay, large enough cap to hold the relay on for 5-10 minutes, maybe it would take too long to charge such a large cap,  or maybe something with a555 timer,  or use a cap and resistor to discharge the cap, maybe a pot to adjust the time, to shut off the MOSFET turning on the relay,  constant or normally close side for the fridge, and the normally open dude for the microwave, preventing the possibility of the fridge switching on while the microwave is in. Use, that may be a good idea. For normal off grid use anyway! Basically a ssr controlled by a cap and resistor,  that controls a relay   sorry to ramble again!! 😆

author: @bruceh4833
text:
Probably one of the best options for switch flippers and button pushers.  A cabin model doesn't need an installer does he?  Keep in mind that ALL heaters can be cabin models and put in a firewalled doghouse outside.  And let's not forget they are mostly exhaust pushers.  Now, that's a problem for close living spaces.  If their exhaust has any gaps in the living space, then the noxious fumes gets pushed by internal pressure out into the home air space.  So, put a bit of aircrete thermal mass near the top of your clandestine chimney and it gets warm enough after starting to draw the exhaust by vacuum instead of the source heat pushing it with internal pressure, so there's not so many worries about gassing sleeping inhabitants.

Of course, expect the traitorous home insurance companies to disqualify wood stoves, etc.  And look forward to your (their) friendly cash-strapped Bobbies to go about with FLIR detection units and ticket books because they are so concerned for your winter European welfare!  Afterall, if they do a really good job with innoculations they can REDUCE the population significantly.  Sir Bill Gates said so hisself!

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
The school camp we used to go to in the 1970s/80s had a diesel water heater. It just dripped diesel onto a angled steel plate above about six inches of space with a chimney drawing out the side near the bottom.
It would heat up about half a ton of water in a couple of hours, enough for 100 kids to have hot showers, and the shed it was in would get to perhaps fifty or more degrees.
About 1 drip per second.
It made no smoke. I'm sure it would have run fine on straight vegetable oils, waste Automotive lubrication oils....

author: @aaronfranklin324
text:
I'm not sure on their output, but I have 3 old-style brass Kero heaters on my yachts. 
Hand pumped pressure starting types. 
I haven't used them because I also have bronze kero/paraffin wick type lamps throughout. And a couple of those provide beautiful light, and quite adequate heating on a fifty foot Wharram Oro.
I have to wonder why it needs electricity at all.

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: The electricity is for running the fan. You need to force air over it to conduct enough heat, otherwise it would 1. melt the whole thing, 2. not be able to provide much heat, or 3. be much larger so it has enough surface area to radiate the space.

replies.author: @aaronfranklin324
replies.text: @DFPercush yeah. Or you could use a thermal siphon with oil cooling. Or get real flash with water  in a phase change heat pipe setup. If you did that you could run a 3 mm bore plastic pipe to a radiator where ever you want the heat, with a 6mm return pipe. You can pipe heat far more efficiently this way. When the water vapour condenses, it releases the latent heat of condensation.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I love the old style heaters

replies.author: @DFPercush
replies.text: @aaronfranklin324 That's kinda what I was thinking earlier in this heating series, to have a burner somewhere on the exterior of a building and a radiator on the inside. Although I was just thinking of a passive water loop. Heat pipes might do the job nicely. Bit more expensive though, and they need to be kept within a certain operating range. I think just piping out the exhaust from a heater like this is a nice solution though. That's the main thing is you don't want your whole house to smell like deisel or kerosene lol. I guess if you wanted to turn it into a passive solution, you could always rip the block off of this one, ditch the fan, surround the block with whatever kind of heat exchanger you have in mind and go from there.

author: @f.d.6667
text: These are awesome little machines! Bought this model last year and put it in my garage so I have an additional heated space for my woodworking projects in winter. Impressed with the quality and the efficiency. Running it on light oil that's normally used for our central heating, so the 5.5kW of the machine cost me about 50cts/hr to run. That's way, WAY cheaper than running an electric space heater... (BTW: thank you, Angela Merkel, for totally screwing up our electricity production!)

replies.author: @nickchloeclaire
replies.text: Can I ask how long yours runs for on 1lt of fuel please.

replies.author: @alexwild4350
replies.text:
@nickchloeclaire Thought I'd reply as I happen to have the manual on the desk right in front of me. There are three models quoted -
2kw = 0.12 - 0.24 | 5kw = 0.18 - 0.48 | 8kw 0.21 - 0.54 Lt/Hr
Obviously the range given is depending on how hard the heater is driven.
The 8kw models actually aren't 8kw, this is the usual optimistic Chinese marketing in operation. The 8kw model is something around 4.5kw, and for those with a mathematical inclination, since the figures above would equate to Btu's per hour, and kilowatts are a unit of electrical power, the conversion and thus comparison can be made.

The reason these heaters have 'exploded' on to the market in recent years is because they are a copy of the Eberspacher, a German heater of the same design, who's patent recently expired. The equivalent German made Eberspacher heater would be in the order of £700 or more.
By size, the Eberspacher model D2 is comparable by physical size to the 8kw Chinese model, and is sold as a 4kw model.

Hope this helps.

replies.author: @f.d.6667
replies.text: @nickchloeclaire It's been a while since I last fired it up (10 months or so) and I run it on a low pump setting (but crank it up to full power say, 15-30 mins before shutting it off) and the usable fuel volume lasts me about "2-3 workshop sessions"... I did the math when the heater was new and calculated 46cts/hr of operating cost back then. No clue how many hours this was ...maybe 8-ish?

author: @Robertnight888
text: So 12 v how many watts and can you run it off the mains by transformer  12v how many amps ?

replies.author: @davidcrozier8341
replies.text: It draws around 12a at 12v on start up when glow plug warms chamber - once burning it draws 1-2a and when you switch it off it uses the glow plug to burn off the fuel in the chamber so draws 12a until it shuts down . I have metered one.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

author: @woodworks2123
text: Nice machine and great price. The outsiders heater is ingeniously simple. Obviously he has a tiny space to heat but with  perhaps a veg oil drip burner or bioethanol or just a paraffin wicking burner for much more heat to the pipe and perhaps a more powerful fan than he had. if you had a side panel next to a back door. You could replace the bottom pane of glass for maybe a bit of cement board. And either exhaust the fumes outside with the unit inside as the outsider did. Or have the whole unit outside so it's safer and have a u shaped pipe coming from the house to the unit then back to the house again taking cooler air from the bottom and pushing the hotter air up higher. Theres not enough time in the day for me to do all the projects I want at once. I'm glad there's someone like you rob inspiring people and testing out ideas.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yes I liked what he did too

author: @peterxyz3541
text: Waste restaurant grease can be sourced for cheap.  Grease is bought by a recycle company and sold to cosmetic companies or soap maker.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that I didn't know!

replies.author: @peterxyz3541
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering from memory, the company paid us under $0.20/gallon for our used fryer grease.  If your local restaurant is willing to work with you, I think it’s more than fair to offer $0.25/gallon for the waste oil👍🏼

author: @dfhepner
text:
The heater is a Chinese copy of a German design. They were made for cabin heaters in cars and trucks. If you buy the original Wabasto heater it will cost you about $1000. 
The 8kw unit is a copy of a 5kw just over clocked. 
There is a UK guy that plays around with these Chinese Diesel Heaters on YT.

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: I actually used mine when my furnace went out in an ice storm last winter

replies.author: @keithbill310
replies.text: How reliable are they and how long does it last before the glow plug needs replacing say for example ..

replies.author: @ProlificInvention
replies.text: @keithbill310 Very reliable in my experience (I have 5 of them) one unit has 3 winters of heating my garage without any maintenance, although I did order a glow plug kit for like $15 that came with new gaskets, several glow plugs and some other parts that I plan on changing out, but not absolutely necessary.

replies.author: @robindumpleton3742
replies.text: @keithbill310 been running mine, this is my third winter. This year when I test fired it in October, the fuel pipe had rotted, so got a yard of tygon 4mm and repaired it, other wise it has run like a dream. In very cold weather, I use it to heat up my mobile home. I run it on either road diesel or paraffin, which ever is cheapest, but I have heard of people running on Canola oil, C2 heating oil, which is kerosine, or even waste oil filtered, you can either build a centrifuge or just filter through a toilet roll. (If no water in the oil the toilet roll does not disintegrate, then mix 50/50 with paraffin or kerosine. Far cheaper than an electric or gas bill. About a tenth of the price

author: @C-M-E
text:
Vevor's quality has come up tremendously in the last few years, largely for the better. I used to view them as the ebay special, maybe you'll get a few uses out of it before it dies. 
Truth be told, they've got a HUGE catalog of stuff and worldwide distribution, still with quite good value when everything seems to be spiraling out of affordability. I've got a lot of their direct pieces and undoubtedly rebranded items (which I'm guessing Vevor also distributes under an umbrella of brands, too.) I don't count that as a strike against them necessarily, even once quality 'branded' names do it.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I have a couple of their bits and like you worried about longevity - but I agree they do a good job for the price
